purpose: Define governance rules for the complete lifecycle of EAFIX atomic modules — from creation through retirement. Recovered from EAFIX_auth_docs/Mod_refactor_plan.txt (deleted in commit 0f2c238).
authority_note: This file governs lifecycle rules only. It does not override module_catalog.json for identity data. Module identities are defined in module_catalog.json exclusively.
core_principle: Treat the module system as a versioned governance system, not a one-time cleanup. The catalog is never treated as final architecture — modules will be added, split, merged, renamed, and retired. The mistake is treating any catalog pass as frozen.

lifecycle_status_definitions.meaning: Mentioned in an MCR; not yet accepted into any catalog pass.
lifecycle_status_definitions.governance_rule: A proposed module has no numeric ID. Use CANDIDATE-<name> notation, not a real ordinal.

lifecycle_status_definitions.meaning: Has enough evidence to evaluate for promotion to canonical.
Accepted into the next catalog pass but not yet locked.
lifecycle_status_definitions.governance_rule: Approved modules receive a numeric_module_id and canonical_symbol.

lifecycle_status_definitions.meaning: Locked in the current catalog pass. This is the normal active state.
lifecycle_status_definitions.governance_rule: A canonical module identity may only change through an accepted MCR.

lifecycle_status_definitions.meaning: Has verified source files, service binding, and runtime binding.
lifecycle_status_definitions.governance_rule: Implementation status is tracked via reconciliation flags (NO_FILES, SERVICE_UNBOUND, etc.).

lifecycle_status_definitions.meaning: Has a valid, non-stale atomic manifest conforming to eafix_unified_atomic_module_schema_v1_0_0.json.
lifecycle_status_definitions.governance_rule: A manifest is downstream of the catalog. Never edit a manifest to resolve architecture questions — those go through MCR first.

lifecycle_status_definitions.meaning: Old symbol retained for traceability. The module entry exists only to preserve historical resolution.
lifecycle_status_definitions.governance_rule: Deprecated aliases are recorded in legacy_aliases field of the surviving module's catalog entry.

lifecycle_status_definitions.meaning: No longer active. The numeric ID and symbol are permanently reserved.
lifecycle_status_definitions.governance_rule: Retired modules keep their entry in the catalog with identity_status: retired and a superseded_by reference.

lifecycle_status_definitions.meaning: Replaced by one or more successor modules.
lifecycle_status_definitions.governance_rule: Superseded modules keep their entry and record superseded_by pointing to the successor numeric IDs.

id_permanence_rules.rule: Numeric module IDs are permanent and are never reused, even after retirement.
details: A module_id (20-digit numeric matching ^5\d{19}$) is assigned once and never reassigned.
Canonical symbols may change through an accepted RENAME_SYMBOL MCR.
Module names may change within limits.
Module purpose may evolve within limits.
When a module is retired: identity_status is set to 'retired'; superseded_by lists the successor numeric IDs; legacy_aliases retains the prior symbol for traceability.
If a module is split: retiring module records superseded_by pointing to the two new modules; new modules each get a new numeric_module_id.
If modules merge: surviving module's ID is kept; retired modules become deprecated_alias entries in the surviving module's legacy_aliases.

change_request_system.description: All architecture modifications must enter through a formal Module Change Request (MCR) before any catalog, crosswalk, manifest, or contract changes are made.

change_type_enum.meaning: Add a new atomic module with a new numeric ID and canonical symbol.
One module becomes two or more modules. Retiring module records superseded_by.
Multiple modules collapse into one surviving module. Retired modules become deprecated_alias entries.
Existing module keeps its identity but loses responsibilities to another module.
Existing module gains responsibility from another module or new area.
Symbol changes; numeric ID remains the same. Old symbol becomes a legacy_alias.
Module stops being active. ID and symbol remain reserved. Entry stays in catalog with status: retired.
A former work cell (bounded sub-unit within a module) becomes a full atomic module.
A documented submodule becomes a full atomic module.
A module or submodule is reduced to an internal function of a parent module.
Logic is reclassified as domain-neutral and relocated to a SK-prefix shared kernel module.
Input or output contracts of a module are being modified.
Source file ownership is transferred between modules.
The owning service, MQL4 component, port, or communication channel of a module changes.
Add a new term to the governance glossary (glossary MCR).
Change the definition of an existing glossary term (glossary MCR).
Mark a glossary term as obsolete (glossary MCR).

three_layer_architecture.description: Module records are maintained in three distinct layers to prevent casual edits to the module list.

layers.purpose: The currently approved flat module list. Single source of truth for module identity.
Records why each module was kept, split, promoted, deferred, merged, or rejected.
Proposed future changes before they are accepted into the catalog. One file per MCR.

catalog_pass_governance.description: The module catalog evolves through versioned passes. Each pass produces a locked catalog snapshot.
pass_sequence: Pass 1 — Initial canonical identification of well-supported modules
Pass 2 — EA-side systems, position sizing, CAL8 registry, event-gateway, stub services, Grafana classification
catalog_pass_governance.single_current_catalog: Only one file is marked as the current catalog at any time, referenced by module_system_index.json. This avoids 'which version should AI use?' problems.

reconciliation_flag_governance.description: Reconciliation flags are treated as migration work queues, not merely defects.

queues.queue_meaning: Needs first manifest and validation evidence

manifest_governance.rule: Manifests are downstream of catalog decisions. Never hand-edit a manifest to resolve architecture arguments — those go through MCR first.
manifest_governance.flow: MCR accepted → decomposition decisions updated → flat module catalog updated → crosswalk updated → affected manifests regenerated or patched → validators run → stale manifests marked
